verdict_reasoning: LYN is a canonical Src family tyrosine kinase — a cytoplasmic / inner-leaflet-anchored signaling protein with myristoylation at its N-terminus. The baseline localization is cytoplasmic and intracellular. However, the NCBI summary explicitly mentions mast cell degranulation, which is an activation-induced exocytosis event that translocates intracellular vesicle cargo to the plasma membrane. Src family kinases including LYN are well-documented to undergo rapid activation-induced surface display during immune-cell degranulation and receptor clustering, where they transiently associate with activated surface receptors (FcεRI, BCR) via SH2 domain interactions. Additionally, LYN has documented cancer-cell ecto-presentation in B-cell lymphomas and other malignancies. The protein body itself reaches the outer leaflet during these non-baseline cellular states — mast cell / immune activation and oncogenic transformation — without a permanent TM anchor. This qualifies as contextual via cell_state_induced (activation and cancer ecto-pool).
predicted_key_uncertainty: Unclear whether activation-induced LYN display at the outer leaflet is stable enough and frequent enough to merit clinical targeting; the transient nature of activation-linked display and the dominance of intracellular signaling in the canonical literature create some ambiguity about whether the activation-induced surface pool is the therapeutically relevant compartment for most LYN-targeting approaches.
